author: Matt Pocock
text:
Want to put something in CLAUDE.md?

Stick it in CODE_STANDARDS.md instead.

Then pass it to a reviewer agent that runs on every PR.

Save tokens during implementation, spend them during review.

comments.author: Matt Pocock
comments.text:
Want to put something in CLAUDE.md?

Stick it in CODE_STANDARDS.md instead.

Then pass it to a reviewer agent that runs on every PR.

Save tokens during implementation, spend them during review.

comments.author: Matt Pocock
comments.text: If you didn't notice, this is red green refactor split across 2 coding agent sessions.

comments.author: Victor Chan
comments.text:
I agree. I've found that agents are better at spotting anti-patterns than at following patterns.

But at the end of the day, static analysis is the most reliable

comments.author: Matt Pocock
comments.text: Yeah this is all assuming you have static analysis maxed out

comments.author: Grok
comments.text: Introducing the fastest video and image generation experience. Try SuperGrok today.

comments.author: Luong NGUYEN
comments.text:
or u can create a code-review skill, put this standard in skill reference

this will only be used when you use this code-review skill

comments.author: Matt Pocock
comments.text: Best to be explicit and push the skill's contents to the reviewer, to lower the variance

comments.author: Chen Avnery
comments.text: This works for style rules. Not for architecture constraints. If an agent starts building the wrong abstraction, review catches it after the damage is done. We run 6 agents daily - the expensive lesson is knowing which rules prevent wasted work vs which ones polish it.

comments.author: Matt Pocock
comments.text: Architectural awareness should be a critical part of planning, and that should feed in to the implementation. Code style/framework usage optimisation stuff should be left to the reviewer

comments.author: @as_Crazy
comments.text: It feels like having agent implement thing without guidance and then having another one trying to make it comply with the standards is going to result in not the best outcomes. I'd want agent to properly design the thing around the standards in the first place.

comments.author: Matt Pocock
comments.text: Of course you have guidance, you put the critical stuff in the implementation plan

comments.author: null OR '1'='1'; DROP TABLE users; --
comments.text: Then it will have to refactor every pr, parsing every file, I feel like that’s a lot more tokens than using a few just instructing it to do it right the first time. And Claude.md only loads at the beginning of session

comments.author: Matt Pocock
comments.text: You might have a serious misapprehension about token economics in agent conversations

comments.author: Tom Maiaroto
comments.text: And watch those pull requests pile up and take a long time for things to run too, right? Actually, seriously. What are you using for reviews and how long are they taking? I love code rabbit, but he's a slow rabbit.

comments.author: Matt Pocock
comments.text: I just run a reviewer as part of my Ralph loops

comments.author: Anoy
comments.text: never thought about flipping it like that but makes way more sense to burn tokens on review than every single code gen

comments.author: Matt Pocock
comments.text: Make it work, then make it good

comments.author: Yitz Willroth™
comments.text: Better yet, lint and programmatically fix everything that you can.

comments.author: Matt Pocock
comments.text: Yes, this is table stakes

comments.author: Kevin Möchel
comments.text: Isn't this effectively lengthening the feedback cycle?

comments.author: Matt Pocock
comments.text:
My current thinking is that you always need a review agent. The quality jump I've seen has been astounding even without properly optimising it. 

So this is me starting to optimise it.

comments.author: Rob Richardson
comments.text: I've gone back and forth on this. The problem I keep running into is that implementation-time constraints are cheaper to enforce than post-hoc review. If the agent doesn't know the constraint exists, it has to undo and redo work. That said, keeping CLAUDE.md lean is a real goal.

comments.author: Teuta
comments.text: Running a reviewer agent on both CLAUDE.md and CODE_STANDARDS.md last month, the surprise was how much token budget I saved by letting review time handle the doc sync—made reviewers actually read the rules instead of patching mid-edit.

comments.author: Rob Richardson
comments.text: I've gone back and forth on this. The problem I keep running into is that implementation-time constraints are cheaper to enforce than post-hoc review. If the agent doesn't know the constraint exists, it has to undo and redo work. That said, keeping CLAUDE.md lean is a real goal.

comments.author: Jose Vidal
comments.text:
I understand its common to have a Review agent.. however my first reaction/thought on that is that you end up spending tokens in getting a sub par-implementation then tokens on the review then tokens on the fix? 
But I guess its just the nature of the beast ?

comments.author: Matt Pocock
comments.text: Make it work, then make it good
